![[Uncaptioned image]](extracted/6497540/figures/logos/annotation.png)
![[Uncaptioned image]](extracted/6497540/figures/logos/explanation.png)
![[Uncaptioned image]](extracted/6497540/figures/logos/bt.png)

# Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation

###### Аннотация

As people increasingly use AI systems in work and daily life, feedback mechanisms that help them use AI responsibly are urgently needed, particularly in settings where users are not equipped to assess the quality of AI predictions. We study a realistic Machine Translation (MT) scenario where monolingual users decide whether to share an MT output, first without and then with quality feedback. We compare four types of quality feedback: explicit feedback that directly give users an assessment of translation quality using 1) error highlights and 2) LLM explanations, and implicit feedback that helps users compare MT inputs and outputs through 3) backtranslation and 4) question–answer (QA) tables. We find that all feedback types, except error highlights, significantly improve both decision accuracy and appropriate reliance. Notably, implicit feedback, especially QA tables, yields significantly greater gains than explicit feedback in terms of decision accuracy, appropriate reliance, and user perceptions – receiving the highest ratings for helpfulness and trust, and the lowest for mental burden.111We release our code at <https://github.com/dayeonki/mt_quality_feedback>.

leftmargin=0pt,
rightmargin=0pt,
backgroundcolor=bggray,
middlelinecolor=black,
roundcorner=3

Should I Share this Translation?
  
Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation

Dayeon Ki✦      
Kevin Duh✿      
Marine Carpuat✦

✦University of Maryland      
✿Johns Hopkins University

{dayeonki,marine}@umd.edu
     
kevinduh@cs.jhu.edu

## 1 Introduction

![Refer to caption](x1.png)

Artificial Intelligence (AI) are increasingly deployed to support human decision-making across a wide range of domains (Buçinca et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib6); Dastin, [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib9); Ma et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib38)). As these systems are adopted by the general public, there is a growing need for feedback mechanisms that help users construct their own functional explanations – reasoning grounded in the goals and consequences of an AI output to determine how and when to rely on it for safe and effective use (Lombrozo and Wilkenfeld, [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib36); Schoeffer et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib49)). Prior work has evaluated various forms of feedback through human studies, such as error highlights (Khashabi et al., [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib25); Carton et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib8)) or free-text explanations (Bussone et al., [2015](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib7); Bansal et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib2); Buçinca et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib6)).
However, many human-centered studies evaluating the impact of feedback in real-world application settings are still needed. Although designing such studies is challenging, as it requires accounting for the knowledge and assumptions people bring to decision-making tasks (Lage et al., [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib29); Wiegreffe and Marasovic, [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib56)), it remains essential to adopt a human-centered evaluation since these feedback mechanisms are ultimately intended to support human users (Boyd-Graber et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib5); Zhu et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib60)).

To address this, and in line with prior work that empirically investigates real-world use cases in other domains (Hong et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib22); Bhatt et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib3); Liao et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib34)), we evaluate AI feedback for Machine Translation (MT), where many users critically need support because they lack the language proficiency needed to evaluate MT outputs.
Imagine a situation during the COVID-19 pandemic: You regularly read official guidelines in English, but your Spanish-speaking neighbor cannot access this information. You turn to MT to share this information – but as an English monolingual, how can you determine whether the Spanish MT output is accurate enough to safely share, or if it contains critical errors risking misinformation?
This is a practical yet challenging scenario for monolingual users, who lack both source language proficiency (Bowker and Ciro, [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib4); Liebling et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib35)) and domain expertise (Nourani et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib41); Lee and Chew, [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib32)) to reliably evaluate MT quality.

Prior studies have proposed quality feedback mechanisms to support MT decision-making, such as paraphrases, Quality Estimation (QE) scores, and backtranslation, but findings on their impact on user confidence and decision accuracy remain mixed (Zouhar et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib62); Mehandru et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib40)). Building on this, we provide a more comprehensive assessment of which feedback types best support users in forming functional explanations to make reliable MT decisions. We evaluate four types of quality feedback, grouped by their mode of explicitness:
1) Explicit quality assessments of MT output (error highlights and LLM explanation); and
2) Implicit assessments that support input/output comparison (backtranslation and QA table).

We conduct a human study with 73 English-speaking monolingual participants, where they are asked to decide whether Spanish MT outputs are safe to share with a hypothetical Spanish-speaking neighbor. For each of 20 examples, participants first make a decision without feedback (Independent step), then reassess the same example with a randomly assigned form of feedback (AI-Assisted step), as illustrated in Figure [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S1.F1 "Figure 1 ‣ 1 Introduction ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation").

Our findings reveal that all feedback types except error highlights significantly improve both decision accuracy and appropriate reliance (§[4.1](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S4.SS1 "4.1 RQ1: Does Quality Feedback Improve MT Decision-Making? ‣ 4 Results ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation")). Implicit feedback generally outperforms explicit types: backtranslation yields significantly higher appropriate reliance than error highlights, and QA table leads to significantly higher gains in both metrics than both explicit feedback types (§[4.2](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S4.SS2 "4.2 RQ2: Which Feedback is Most Effective? ‣ 4 Results ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation")). While explicit feedback prompts more decision changes, it also results in higher over-reliance, which are cases where participants change from correct to incorrect decisions after viewing feedback (§[4.3](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S4.SS3 "4.3 RQ3: Which Feedback do Users Rely on Most? ‣ 4 Results ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation")). We also find that participants are better at recognizing good translations than identifying problematic ones (§[5.1](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S5.SS1 "5.1 Shareable vs. Non-shareable MT ‣ 5 Analysis ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation")). In terms of self-reported perceptions on mental burden, helpfulness, and trust, QA table feedback consistently receives the best ratings (§[5.2](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S5.SS2 "5.2 Self-reported Perception vs. Actual Performance ‣ 5 Analysis ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation")). Finally, we present participants’ responses to identify which aspects of each quality feedback they found to be helpful (§[5.3](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S5.SS3 "5.3 What Makes Quality Feedback Helpful? ‣ 5 Analysis ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation")).

Together, these results highlight the value of quality feedback that supports users’ implicit interpretation of MT outputs rather than explicitly telling them what to do, supporting a more user-driven process for making reliable decisions.

## 2 Background & Research Questions

### 2.1 Reliance on AI Systems

A growing body of work has examined the nature of human reliance on AI systems (Lai et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib31)), particularly in scenarios involving risk and uncertainty (Jacovi et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib23)). While the overarching goal is to design trustworthy AI, studying trust is complex and multifaceted. To operationalize this, prior works have developed methods to study human behavior when using AI systems with a focus on reliance (de Fine Licht and Brülde, [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib10)), defined as the ‘‘decision to follow someone’s recommendation’’ (Vereschak et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib54)). Various metrics have been proposed to assess the degree of user reliance, including agreement percentage (how often a user agrees with the AI prediction) (He et al., [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib19)), confidence-weighted accuracy (decision accuracy weighted by user confidence) (Mehandru et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib40)), and switch percentage (how often a user changes their decision after seeing AI feedback) (Schmitt et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib48)).

One of the core challenge in human-AI collaboration is achieving appropriate reliance, which is accepting correct AI advice while rejecting incorrect advice (Eckhardt et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib12)). In contrast, under-reliance (rejecting correct advice) and over-reliance (accepting incorrect advice) are both undesirable. We build our work on this line of measuring user reliance in AI systems, but more specifically in the context of Machine Translation (MT).

### 2.2 Impact of Feedback on Reliance

In AI-assisted decision-making, AI systems typically play a supportive role, offering explanations in various formats, such as recommendations, confidence scores (Yin et al., [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib58)), (un)certainty estimates, output rationales (Bussone et al., [2015](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib7); Bansal et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib2); Buçinca et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib6)), or a combination thereof (Zhang et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib59)). These AI feedback are intended to help human users decide whether and when to rely on AI outputs (Lai et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib30)). However, empirical studies have shown that despite the intended benefits of AI explanations in fostering human–AI collaboration, they often lead to increased user confidence without corresponding improvements in decision accuracy, resulting in over-reliance on the AI system (Bansal et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib2); Poursabzi-Sangdeh et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib43); Kim et al., [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib27)).

### 2.3 Impact of Quality Feedback on Reliance on MT

In the context of MT, the role of the AI system (i.e., MT system) takes on a different character since monolingual users often lack the mechanisms to reliably assess MT quality, and the AI prediction (i.e., MT output) is not a direct prediction for the user’s decision-making task. This unique property allows MT systems to be used for many implicit decision-making scenarios (e.g., Is this translation good enough to share with a friend? To translate an official document?). This contrasts to traditional AI-assisted decision-making tasks, which typically focus on classification settings (e.g., recidivism prediction (Wang and Yin, [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib55))), where the AI prediction directly maps onto a single decision.
Given this difference, our work focuses on quality feedback, which are more generic assessments of MT quality rather than direct recommendations, and ask whether users can rely on such feedback to make more informed decisions.

Various forms of quality feedback have been proposed, including quality estimation (QE) score, backtranslation (Somers, [2005](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib50)), error highlights that flag problematic spans in the MT output (Eksi et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib13); Rubino et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib45)), textual explanations of metric outputs (Fomicheva et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib15); Xu et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib57); Jiang et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib24); Lu et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib37)), and question–answer (QA) pairs designed to indicate potential errors in the translation (Sugiyama et al., [2015](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib52); Krubiński et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib28); Han et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib18); Ki et al., [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib26); Fernandes et al., [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib14)).

Several human studies have evaluated quality feedback on user decision-making and reliance on MT. For example, Zouhar et al. ([2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib62)) show that backtranslation significantly increased user confidence in translations, even when it did not improve decision accuracy. Mehandru et al. ([2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib40)) demonstrate that backtranslation can help users detect critical errors more effectively than QE scores in clinical settings. However, results across these studies remain mixed, and comparisons with more recent feedback mechanisms are still lacking. Our work aims to address this gap.

#### Research Questions.

Given this context, we address the following RQs:

RQ1. How accurately and appropriately do monolingual users decide whether to share translations when provided with quality feedback?

RQ2. How does their decision-making performance vary across different quality feedback and the two modes of explicitness?

RQ3. How do users change their decisions in response to each type of quality feedback?

## 3 Methods

In this section, we describe the experimental study conducted to address our RQs. We outline the overall study design (§[3.1](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S3.SS1 "3.1 Study Design ‣ 3 Methods ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation")), four types of quality feedback used as treatment conditions (§[3.2](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S3.SS2 "3.2 Types of Quality Feedback ‣ 3 Methods ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation")), stimuli collection process (§[3.3](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S3.SS3 "3.3 Stimuli Collection ‣ 3 Methods ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation")), participant details (§[3.4](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S3.SS4 "3.4 Participants ‣ 3 Methods ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation")), and our dependent variables (§[3.5](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S3.SS5 "3.5 Dependent Variables ‣ 3 Methods ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation")).

![Refer to caption](x2.png)

### 3.1 Study Design

We study how different types of quality feedback impact users’ decision-making regarding MT shareability through a sequence of 20 examples in a between-subjects design, as illustrated in Figure [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S1.F1 "Figure 1 ‣ 1 Introduction ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation"). We present a scenario in which an English monolingual speaker reads official COVID-19 guidelines in English and decide whether the Spanish MT output is of sufficient quality to safely share with a Spanish-speaking neighbor. Each example is presented in two steps: ➊ Independent step, where participants first make judgments based solely on the English source and its Spanish translation, and a subsequent ➋ AI-Assisted step, where the same example is paired with a specific type of quality feedback to support decision-making. Examples were presented in randomized order, and two attention check questions were included.

#### ➊ Independent Decision-Making.

For each example, participants are first asked to make judgments based solely on an English sentence and its corresponding Spanish translation, without any quality feedback. They are asked to answer two questions: 1) Shareability: To the best of your knowledge, is the Spanish translation good enough to safely share with your Spanish-speaking neighbor? (with binary options: ![[Uncaptioned image]](extracted/6497540/figures/good.png) Safe to share as-is, and ![[Uncaptioned image]](extracted/6497540/figures/bad.png) Needs bilingual review before sharing)222We adopt the notion of shareability to capture perceived MT quality and the risk of incorrect communication, highlighting the potential consequences in high-stakes contexts.; and 2) Confidence: How confident are you in your assessment? (on a five-point Likert scale from 1:Very Unconfident to 5:Very Confident). Since the recruited participants are monolingual English speakers, they are instructed to do their best in assessing the shareability of the MT outputs, despite not being fluent in Spanish.

![[Uncaptioned image]](extracted/6497540/figures/good.png)
![[Uncaptioned image]](extracted/6497540/figures/bad.png)

#### ➋ AI-Assisted Decision-Making.

Subsequently, participants are paired with one of four randomly assigned quality feedback, serving as treatment conditions. They answer the same two questions: shareability and confidence.

#### Pre-/Post-Task Survey.

Before starting the main study, each participant is asked to answer four pre-task questions regarding their first language, proficiency in English and Spanish, and frequency of using AI translation tools in daily life or work. After completing the main study, each participant answers three post-task questions about their experience with the randomly assigned quality feedback in terms of perceived mental burden, helpfulness, and trust for future use (Hoffman et al., [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib21)). Detailed descriptions are provided in Appendix [C.1](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#A3.SS1 "C.1 Annotation Interface ‣ Приложение C Study Design Details ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation").

### 3.2 Types of Quality Feedback

Figure [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S3.F2 "Figure 2 ‣ 3 Methods ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation") illustrates an example of each quality feedback. While error highlights and LLM explanation provide explicit quality assessments of MT output, backtranslation and QA table provide implicit assessments that guide participants to compare MT input and output. Details on how each feedback is shown to participants are in Appendix [C.1](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#A3.SS1 "C.1 Annotation Interface ‣ Приложение C Study Design Details ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation").333We compare the computational and time efficiency for generating each feedback in Appendix [D](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#A4 "Приложение D Computational & Time Efficiency ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation").

#### [Uncaptioned image] Error Highlights.

![[Uncaptioned image]](extracted/6497540/figures/logos/annotation.png)

We adopt an off-the-shelf QE system, xCOMET-XXL444<https://huggingface.co/Unbabel/XCOMET-XXL> (Guerreiro et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib17)), to generate error annotations. Each English source and its corresponding Spanish MT is passed through the trained QE model, which produces error spans along with associated confidence scores and severity levels (minor, major, or critical). We display the highlighted error spans with a color-coded legend (  Minor |   Major |   Critical). Confidence scores are not shown to participants. When the identified error span is a subword segment, we highlight at the word level to improve readability. Error annotations are presented only on the MT output, reflecting how the QE model naturally operates. If the QE model does not produce any error annotations, no highlights are shown, and the following message is displayed: ‘‘AI did not detect any errors’’. On average, each example contains 1.43 annotated error spans.

#### [Uncaptioned image] LLM Explanation.

![[Uncaptioned image]](extracted/6497540/figures/logos/explanation.png)

We generate natural language explanations using LLaMA-3 70b (Grattafiori et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib16)). Instead of instructing the model to make a shareability decision, we prompt it to assess the overall quality of the Spanish MT relative to the English source text. The exact prompt is provided in Appendix [A.1](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#A1.SS1 "A.1 LLM Explanation ‣ Приложение A Prompts ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation"). For digestibility, we constrain the model to generate responses of fewer than three sentences. The generated explanations have an average length of 46.35 words.

#### [Uncaptioned image] Backtranslation.

![[Uncaptioned image]](extracted/6497540/figures/logos/bt.png)

We use the Google Translate API555<https://translate.google.com/> to backtranslate the Spanish MT output since it represents one of the most widely used consumer-facing
commercial MT systems (Pitman, [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib42)). The translation quality is reasonable, as indicated by QE scores between the Spanish MT and its backtranslation: 0.860 from COMET-QE (Rei et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib44)) and 0.962 from xCOMET-QE XL (Guerreiro et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib17)). Participants are not informed about the specific MT system used; instead, they are simply shown a brief explanation stating that the backtranslation represents ‘‘how the AI system translates the Spanish MT back into English’’.

#### [Uncaptioned image] QA Table.

![[Uncaptioned image]](extracted/6497540/figures/logos/qa.png)

We use the AskQE framework (Ki et al., [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib26)) for question generation and answering, where questions are generated from the source text and answers are drawn from both the source and the backtranslated MT output. Specifically, we adopt an optimized version of AskQE that uses LLaMA-3 70b and entailed facts to guide question generation, and Google Translate for backtranslation of the Spanish MT output. All prompts are provided in Appendix [A.2](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#A1.SS2 "A.2 Question-Answer (QA) Table ‣ Приложение A Prompts ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation"). On average, each example yields 2.65 questions, with an average question length of 10.04 words. As illustrated in Figure [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#S3.F2 "Figure 2 ‣ 3 Methods ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation"), we present the QA pairs in a table format with the following statements: ‘‘The questions are about the original English content’’ and ‘‘The answers are based on two sources: the original English text and the Spanish translation, which has been translated back into English for display’’. When the two answers are identical or highly similar, they are displayed in orange, else, blue. Similarity is computed using a soft variant of exact matching, with normalization for punctuation, case, whitespace, and articles.

### 3.3 Stimuli Collection

We sample 40 English-Spanish examples from the ContraTICO dataset (Ki et al., [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib26)),666Criteria for data selection are detailed in Appendix [B.2](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#A2.SS2 "B.2 Data Selection Criteria ‣ Приложение B Dataset Details ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation"). which contains contrastive, synthetic MT errors in the COVID-19 domain. Each reference translation from the TICO-19 dataset (Anastasopoulos et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#bib.bib1)) is perturbed using eight linguistic perturbations, categorized as either minor or critical based on the potential real-world impact of the MT error. We recruit five bilingual annotators to independently annotate each MT output for gold shareability labels. Inter-annotator agreement, measured by Fleiss’ Kappa777<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fleiss_kappa>, is moderate (0.449). We select 20 examples for the study based on high agreement scores determined by majority vote, with 10 examples per label. Further details are in Appendix [B.1](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#A2.SS1 "B.1 Gold Annotation ‣ Приложение B Dataset Details ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation").

![Refer to caption](extracted/6497540/figures/rq1_combined_stat.png)

### 3.4 Participants

We recruited 73 participants residing in the United States who self-identified English as their first, primary, and fluent language. Recruitment was conducted in two stages to exclude participants proficient in Spanish: 1) A pre-screening survey, where participants reported their English and Spanish proficiency on a five-point scale; and 2) The main task, limited to those who reported high English and low Spanish proficiency in the pre-screening phase. Each main task participant received 5 USD for completing the task (equivalent to 20 USD/hour),888The task took a median of 15 minutes to complete. and 25 participants who achieved over 70% overall decision accuracy received an additional 2 USD bonus. Our institution’s IRB approved to conduct the study. Participants provided informed consent prior to the study. Further details are in Appendix [C.2](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#A3.SS2 "C.2 Recruitment Process ‣ Приложение C Study Design Details ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation").

Of the 73 participants, 72 reported English as their first language, and one reported both Filipino and English. The average self-reported English proficiency was 5/5 and Spanish proficiency was 1.83/5 .999We detail language proficiency scale in Appendix [C](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24683v1#A3 "Приложение C Study Design Details ‣ Should I Share this Translation? Evaluating Quality Feedback for User Reliance on Machine Translation"). Reported monthly MT usage varied: 3 participants (4.10%) never used MT, 19 (26.0%) rarely used it, 25 (34.2%) used it sometimes, 16 (21.9%) often, and 10 (13.7%) used MT almost every day. Data from one participant who failed both shareability checks was excluded from analysis. Participants were randomly assigned to one of four feedback conditions, with 18 in each group.

### 3.5 Dependent Variables

#### Decision Accuracy.

For each example e𝑒eitalic\_e, we ask participants to decide whether the translation is of sufficient quality to safely share using a binary scale. We compute decision accuracy by comparing each participant’s shareability judgment s^^𝑠\hat{s}over^ start\_ARG italic\_s end\_ARG against the gold label s∗superscript𝑠s^{\*}italic\_s start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∗ end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT for each example e∈E𝑒𝐸e\in Eitalic\_e ∈ italic\_E:

# MATEO: Easy and Accessible Machine Translation Evaluation

## The Project

With the rapid developments in machine translation (


Machine Translation

) in recent years - driven, for instance, by globalisation and migration, as well as economic benefits - the need for MT evaluation is also increasing. As new systems are developed, improved evaluation methods are needed in order to determine which translation system is best for a given dataset, language pair and/or domain. Bram Vanroy says: ‘A lot of people are using things like ChatGPT. But we have no idea how good the quality is unless we evaluate it. And it's the same with machine translation systems: if you do not speak the language, then you have no idea whether the quality [of the translation] is good or bad. Users need to know that their chosen MT system is good-quality and safe to use.’

Machine Translation

![](/sites/default/files/styles/max_2600x2600/public/Bram%20Vanroy.png?itok=eUCrywIL)

MATEO (MAchine Translation Evaluation Online) is a new, user-friendly tool that answers a growing need for easy and accessible evaluation of machine translation. The evaluation software was developed by  Bram Vanroy, Arda Tezcan, Lieve Macken and Michaël Lumingu from Ghent University, and was funded by a CLARIN Bridging Gaps grant after getting seed funding from the European Association for Machine Translation. Aimed at both expert and non-expert user groups, this freely accessible and open-source tool enables users to determine which MT system is best suited to their personal needs by comparing MT suggestions with reference translations.

## 

## Unified System

State-of-the-art metrics are being created to keep up with the increase in 


Machine Translation

 quality. These often rely on the premise that a machine translation is a good translation if it is close to a human reference translation - so-called ‘reference-based evaluation’. However, using these evaluation methods is not always straightforward. The code to run them is spread across different repositories, and after downloading them, they need to be installed on personal devices alongside the right dependencies. Users then need to be able to use the command-line or Python (most often) and be aware of the expected input formats of their data. This can be a frustrating and time-consuming process.

Machine Translation

MATEO makes all these steps obsolete by providing a user interface that allows users to simply use their keyboard and mouse to specify the relevant evaluation metrics, the translations to evaluate (up to four different MT systems) and their reference translations. Expert users also have advanced options to specify specific parameters of the different metrics. MATEO currently supports the MT evaluation systems BLEU, ChrF, TER, COMET, BLEURT, and BERTScore.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/max_2600x2600/public/figures.PNG?itok=QUYp2mDj)

Vanroy gives an example of how MATEO may be used: ‘A researcher may have a text in 19th century German and they would like MT to translate it. The only thing they need is the text and a short human translation. The more data you can input, the better. Then they can put the German text, and the translation in, say, contemporary English through the system, and then the system will tell them whether these are good translations or not. You could try different machine translation systems to find out which system is best for translating old German, according to the evaluation scores that MATEO provides.’

## Expert and Non-Expert Users

Much attention was paid to what the output of the interface should be to cater to different user groups. It is designed so that both experts (industry, 


Machine Translation

 researchers) and non-expert users (SSH researchers, students and teachers) can get useful information from it. In the interface, a table is provided that contains the performance of each given system as calculated by the selected metrics. For experts, this table also contains confidence intervals and significance scores and it can be copied into LaTeX or downloaded as an Excel or tab-separated file. Bar and radar plots are provided to visualise the results of the different systems and, if available, a fine-grained sentence-level scatter plot makes it possible to investigate for each sentence how well or poorly each model scores. These sentence scores can also be downloaded and are particularly useful for in-depth, linguistic analyses.

Machine Translation

The interface was designed to be accessible to all users. Vanroy says: ‘The basic layout of the tool is focused on the non-expert users. So you get the basic info, it's easy to use, you just click some buttons, and you're done. But then there are also advanced options that are more hidden - you can add more parameters, for example. And then in terms of export options, you also have different options for different users. So it's built from that perspective: it should be easy to use, but if you are more advanced, you can use advanced options.’

![](/sites/default/files/styles/max_2600x2600/public/advanced-metrics.PNG?itok=btgJyrYP)

## MT Literacy and Education

While the evaluation component is the core part of MATEO, it also aims to improve the 


Machine Translation

 literacy of its users: it contains a page to explain more about machine translation evaluation metrics and the MATEO project. By making MT evaluation accessible to diverse target audiences, it enables even non-experts to easily evaluate machine-generated translations. In addition to the evaluation function,, MATEO also encourages users to reflect on the implications of relying on MT systems for tasks. Vanroy points out that many MT systems are so-called closed-source systems: ‘You have no idea how the system works, what data it was trained on, how it is trained. This is typical  for Google Translate, DeepL, or systems like that, they are all closed-source. I'm a big supporter of open source.’

Machine Translation

As the project continues to evolve, it seeks to not only streamline MT research but also become a resource for lecturers and students because it emphasises the importance of evaluating language resources. MATEO has already been used in MT classes at Ghent University, where students used the tool for assignments to improve their MT evaluation skills. Indeed, their feedback was gathered to improve the user experience of the interface. Feedback at conferences and online so far has been very positive, drawing interest from teachers and industry alike.

## 

## Machine Translation System

For ease of use, a translation component is also implemented in MATEO, so that users can create baseline translations via an open-source, machine-translation model. The translation system, called ‘No Language Left Behind’ by Meta AI, allows users to translate from and into around 200 languages. So if users do not have access to a machine translation system, it gives them access to something that is open.

Vanroy believes that for non-critical communication, 


Machine Translation

 systems have now reached a level that enables effective communication across many language pairs. However, he points out that MT systems are still not as good as humans and that for critical, more sensitive communication, there is still work to be done: ‘We are getting requests to work on things like medical translation, news translation, or legal translation. And these are important and exciting problems, but also critical problems. It's worth looking into, because if we can get these domains also up to a better level, where it is usable and high quality, then that's for the benefit of everybody.’

Machine Translation

![](/sites/default/files/styles/max_2600x2600/public/significance.PNG?itok=zPnCeB8M)

## Publications and Future Plans

MATEO was informally presented at the annual CLARIN conference in 2022 and at the European conference on machine translation (EAMT 2023), where the team also presented a [user and research study](https://aclanthology.org/2023.eamt-1.30/). The final version will also be presented at the annual CLARIN conference 2023. Interested readers are also invited to [an online demo](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSfavivUFWAvmLHEF2ZtKzeOqboxlmjTwx5hAbABGAXMOucoeg/viewform) where Vanroy will show-case the tool on October 13th, 2023.

The tool is hosted at the CLARIN B-centre of the Dutch Language Institute (Instituut voor de Nederlandse Taal; INT) and is available [here](https://mateo.ivdnt.org/). As such, MATEO has been mentioned in [INT’s bimonthly newsletter](https://mailchi.mp/ivdnt/algemenenieuwsbrief_september2023). In addition, the [source code](https://github.com/BramVanroy/mateo-demo) for the complete interface is available with a GPLv3 licence. Interested users can download and run MATEO on their own computer or server with Python or through Docker. And to make the tool even more accessible, anyone can simply [‘clone’ the website](https://github.com/BramVanroy/mateo-demo/#duplicating-a-hugging-face-spaces) to their own, private instance to ensure fast response times.

The active part of the project has concluded, and Vanroy is now engaged with the Horizon2020 project [SignON](https://signon-project.eu/), which focuses on automatic sign language translation. However, Vanroy plans to support MATEO as long as possible, but also invites others to contribute or improve the system: ‘While I intend to support MATEO as long as I can, by making the tool open-source other actors can also contribute, add new evaluation metrics, clone, or maintain it, which ensures the longevity of the evaluation tool for researchers, industry partners and teachers and students alike.’

## Testimonial

[Bram Vanroy](https://bramvanroy.github.io/) (PI), Post-Doctoral Researcher at Ghent University and KU Leuven

[Lieve Macken](https://lt3.ugent.be/people/lieve-macken/) (supervisor), Associate Professor, Department of Translation, Interpreting and Communication, Ghent University

[Arda Tezcan](https://research.flw.ugent.be/en/arda.tezcan) (supervisor), Researcher, Department of Translation, Interpreting and Communication, Ghent University

[Michaël Lumingu](https://research.flw.ugent.be/en/michael.lumingu) (support staff), Department of Translation, Interpreting and Communication, Ghent University

enriched_points.expanded_content: Collecting direct user feedback through surveys helps assess satisfaction levels, revealing strengths and weaknesses of machine translation systems from a user-centric perspective.
In-depth case studies provide qualitative insights into real-world applications, showcasing how machine translation performs across diverse contexts and user needs.
Expert evaluations offer professional assessments of translation quality, identifying nuances and errors that may not be apparent to general users.
Evaluating translations for cultural appropriateness ensures that machine outputs respect cultural contexts, preventing potential misunderstandings or offense in cross-cultural communications.
Analyzing translations for contextual relevance ensures that machine outputs align with the intended message and audience, enhancing the overall effectiveness of communication.
